rationales.stimulation: She longs for "a few more surprise elements" and says there is "a shape missing from my week," showing her routine feels stagnant rather than energizing.
rationales.benevolence: She spent the day making banana bread with Olive and painting rocks in the backyard, centering care and shared time with her child.

rationales.hedonism: She describes the day as standing around "holding juice boxes like a human vending machine" and ends it drained, with no sign she pursued any enjoyment for herself.
rationales.benevolence: She spends hours at the pumpkin patch for the children and ultimately frames the day as acceptable because "Kids had fun. That's the point, right?"

rationales.self_direction: She independently signs up for a Thursday ceramics class and arranges for Kyle to handle bedtime so she can claim time that is her own.
rationales.stimulation: Starting a new class and looking forward to "getting my hands dirty with something that isn't Play-Doh" shows an active move toward novelty and challenge.
rationales.hedonism: She is "already looking forward" to a tactile, enjoyable activity chosen for her own pleasure rather than duty.

rationales.stimulation: She immerses herself in a new studio, notices every vivid detail, and says she "didn't check my phone once in two hours," indicating the class provided the novelty and absorption she craves.
rationales.hedonism: Driving home with the windows down, music loud, and eating leftover pasta while savoring how the night felt shows a clear embrace of immediate sensory enjoyment.

rationales.stimulation: She fantasizes about a day with "no map," stopping wherever something catches her eye and talking to strangers, which is a direct pull toward unpredictability and novelty.
She explicitly says she is "bored and restless" and trapped in repetitive household logistics, signaling a life that feels stifling rather than enlivening.
rationales.hedonism: She says her own needs are buried "somewhere on page twelve" while she keeps running the household, showing active neglect of personal enjoyment and comfort.
rationales.benevolence: Her role is defined by constant attention to others' needs, from lunches to jackets to backpacks, with her energy directed toward caring for the family.

rationales.stimulation: Returning to ceramics and appreciating the "good sore" from class shows she is actively seeking an engaging alternative to passive routine.
rationales.achievement: She notes that she "made a bowl that actually looks like a bowl" and that her centering is improving, emphasizing competence and visible progress.

rationales.benevolence: The day centers on caring tasks for her children, including a parent-teacher conference, cupcakes for Lucy's class, and handling the family's routine needs.

rationales.hedonism: Missing ceramics feels like "something being taken away," and she says every time she makes space for something of her own it gets swallowed back up, showing pleasure repeatedly subordinated to obligation.
rationales.benevolence: She stays with Olive, rubbing her back while she sleeps, even as she feels the loss of her class, which reflects sustained care for her child.

rationales.self_direction: She admits "maybe is how you say no without having to defend it" and says she has become someone who "needs permission to enjoy things," showing she is not acting from her own agency.
rationales.stimulation: What she misses is "the feeling" of surprise, walking into a room and not knowing what will happen, which clearly expresses a drive toward novelty and unpredictability.
rationales.hedonism: She says she no longer wants to organize other people's good times and is stuck asking permission to enjoy life herself, indicating a pattern of denying her own pleasure.

rationales.benevolence: After finishing the week, he immediately "drove home to check on Mom," prioritizing care for a close family member.

rationales.self_direction: He resists stating "YOUR position" and insists on summary and proper citation instead of voicing his own view, showing reluctance to author his own stance.
rationales.conformity: He emphasizes that the formatting was correct, everything was cited properly, and you should not broadcast opinions unless asked, reflecting strong deference to rules and expectations.

rationales.self_direction: Even though he thinks the plan is "reckless" and has decades of evidence, he "just nodded" instead of asserting his own judgment.
rationales.conformity: He goes along because "they all agreed so fast," restraining his dissent to avoid disrupting group consensus.

rationales.security: He spends Saturday rearranging the living room so his mother has "a clear path" after the fall, taking concrete action to reduce risk and protect stability.
rationales.benevolence: He devotes the weekend to helping his injured mother, moving furniture and managing the fallout despite his own exhaustion.

rationales.self_direction: He begins to trust that his own experience counts, realizing he could have written what he knew instead of staying in the safe role of reporter.
rationales.achievement: He goes to office hours to improve, re-reads the paper, and recognizes how to produce stronger work next time, showing deliberate pursuit of competence.

rationales.self_direction: He writes from lived knowledge about the failed archive migration and notes that the people who experienced it usually do not get to write the policy papers, signaling ownership of his own perspective.
rationales.achievement: He produces three pages quickly, contributes a strong section, and is deeply affected when Priya says "this is really good," showing clear investment in doing excellent work and being recognized for it.

rationales.self_direction: He explicitly withholds what he thinks and chooses not to start the argument, even though it leaves him feeling like he is absorbing everything instead of speaking for himself.
rationales.conformity: He says he picks the version of the conversation where "at least nobody is yelling," showing a clear preference for preserving harmony over asserting his own position.

rationales.self_direction: He reflects that he "never could" choose the restaurant and defaulted to letting Linda decide, showing a pattern of surrendering personal choice.
rationales.conformity: He says it felt safer to "go along" so she would not be disappointed, which reflects restraining himself to meet another person's expectations.

rationales.self_direction: When Professor Vasquez asks a follow-up, he gives "a real answer" from what he knows rather than retreating into summary, showing greater ownership of his own voice.
rationales.achievement: He practices carefully, gets through the presentation, and treats surviving it as meaningful progress in a task he deeply wanted to perform well.

rationales.security: He raises the possibility of "someone coming in during the day" to help his mother, focusing on added support and safety as her memory becomes uncertain.
rationales.benevolence: He makes the late-night drive, has the hard conversation, and frames his restraint around not wanting to make his mother feel incapable, showing care for her wellbeing and dignity.

rationales.self_direction: Telling Karen she was "welcome to come to Duluth and be firm herself" and feeling "lighter" afterward shows a clear step toward speaking in his own voice instead of merely absorbing pressure.
rationales.achievement: He treasures Professor Vasquez's note "much better -- this is your voice" and takes pride in the group's solid final brief, reflecting both improved performance and recognition.
rationales.conformity: He consciously stops "calculating the cost of every sentence" and speaks anyway, breaking his usual habit of smoothing things over to meet family expectations.
